title: Postwar
subtitle: A History of Europe Since 1945
author: Tony Judt
description: A sweeping, authoritative history of Europe from the aftermath of World War II through the first decade of the 21st century — examining reconstruction, Cold War divisions, the rise and fall of ideologies, and the making of modern Europe.
originalTitle: Postwar: A History of Europe Since 1945
editionNote: Penguin Press, 2005 (first edition). Updated paperback edition, Penguin, 2010. ISBN 9780142003081 covers the Penguin paperback.
themes: The Reconstruction of Europe After WWII
The Marshall Plan and American Hegemony
Soviet Totalitarianism and the Eastern Bloc
The Rise of the European Welfare State
Cold War Culture Wars and Intellectual Ferment
Decolonization and the End of European Empires
The Fall of the Berlin Wall and German Unification
The Emergence of the European Union
Yugoslav Wars and the Return of Ethnic Violence
Velvet Revolutions of Central and Eastern Europe
Philosophy as a Political Force: From Sartre to Arendt
The Legacy of Totalitarianism and Historical Memory
The Failure of European Leftist Political Traditions
excerpt: In monumental scope and analytical depth, Tony Judt's Postwar is the definitive single-volume history of Europe from 1945 to the early 2000s. Drawing on decades of scholarship and personal witness, Judt traces how a devastated continent rebuilt its economies, invented the welfare state, wrestled with the moral weight of collaboration and resistance, divided and then reunified under Cold War pressures, and finally reimagined itself as a supranational European project. Unflinching in its assessment of communism's record in the East and equally critical of Western complacency, the book argues that Europe's postwar identity was forged not in triumph but in the unresolved tension between memory and forgetting — making it essential reading for understanding the continent that shaped the modern world.
authorBio: Tony Judt (1948–2010) was a British-American historian, essayist, and public intellectual. Educated at King's College, Cambridge, and the École Normale Supérieure in Paris, he taught at the University of California, Berkeley, before settling at New York University, where he founded the Remarque Institute. Diagnosed with ALS in 2008, he continued to write and teach until his death in 2010. His essays on politics and contemporary affairs appeared in The New York Review of Books, The Guardian, and elsewhere.
